title: Lesson 386
transcript: Okay, this is a quick video on MIME headers. What is MIME? MIME stands for Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension Headers. So this protocol was originally designed to allow to basically understand the nature and format of a document file being sent. So the MIME headers identify things like, is this a text document? Is it an HTML document? Is it a JPEG file, et cetera. And as you can tell, multipurpose internet mail extensions. This was originally designed for email, but it's used in lots of other protocols, such as email hypertext transfer protocol for web pages, things like that. That basically indicates the nature and format of a document being transmitted. So now we know what MIME headers are, basically just a simple way of identifying what is being sent. Let's now talk about SMIME. And as you can probably guess, the S in SMIME stands for Secure Multipurpose Internet Mail Exchanges. And what SMIME is, is it's an extension that allows for the secure transmission of this MIME data. And SMIME uses both digital signatures and digital certificates. So FMIME is kind of an integral part of PKI's public key infrastructures. And the reason that S-MIME uses both digital certificates and digital signatures are for the exact reasons that you would think. The digital certificates are being used to authenticate who you're talking to and provide the public key. And the digital signatures are being used to provide the integrity and the authenticity of some data that's being sent in, say, the MIME header. That's all you really need to know about SMIME. It allows you to securely transmit this useful information, and through the use of digital certificates and digital signatures, you can verify the authenticity and the integrity of the MIME data that's being sent.
